message.content:
You are a senior software architect researching 2026 best practices. Research CURRENT (2025-2026) professional naming conventions for a serious production codebase. Specifically: why marketing/temporal adjectives in file and symbol names are a code smell (honest, final, real, true, smart, magic, new, old, v2/v3, r2/r3, sweep, ultimate). What the accepted conventions are (PEP 8, Google Python style, "name by what it IS/DOES not by a quality claim or a version"), how mature teams handle versioned modules (avoid _v2 suffixes; use packages/interfaces), and a concrete renaming discipline. Cite style guides.

Return a tight, concrete markdown brief titled "## Best practice (2026): Naming discipline (no marketing adjectives)" — patterns, named tools, and what "good" looks like. No fluff.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Based on the search results, here's information about PEP 8 and Google Python style guide naming conventions regarding descriptive names:

## PEP 8 Naming Conventions

Names that are visible to the user as public parts of the API should follow conventions that reflect usage rather than implementation. The key principle is emphasizing descriptiveness:

- PEP 8 suggests using lowercase with underscores for functions and variables (snake case), camel case for classes, and uppercase with underscores for constants.
- The best way to name your objects in Python is to use descriptive names to make it clear what the object represents.

## Google Python Style Guide

The Google Python Style Guide has a specific recommendation about descriptive names:

Prefer PEP8-compliant descriptive_names for public APIs, which are much more likely to be encountered out of context. This emphasizes that descriptive names should be prioritized over short or abbreviated names, especially for code that will be used publicly.

## Why Avoid Qualifiers

While the search results don't explicitly discuss avoiding qualifiers, they do emphasize the importance of descriptive, meaningful names. Variable names should clearly describe what the data represents, not just store a value. This principle implies that names should focus on describing the actual purpose and content rather than adding unnecessary qualifiers.

The general guidance across both PEP 8 and the Google style guide is to prioritize clarity and descriptiveness in naming, making code more readable and maintainable.

SomethingManager","url":"https://blog.codinghorror.com/i-shall-call-it-somethingmanager/"},{"title":"Code Smells: Naming","url":"https://iafrifa.hashnode.dev/code-smells-naming"},{"title":"Good Code: Naming is important and helps us find code smells | by Sunny Mattas | The Startup | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/swlh/good-code-naming-is-important-and-helps-us-find-code-smells-c9c8c65b8698"}]

Based on the search results, I found relevant information about code smell naming patterns. Here's what I found:

## Code Smell Naming Patterns

The names "Manager" and "Helper" in classes are considered name smells. Specifically:

**Manager**: The name "Manager" in a class is often part of an effort to deodorize a code smell like "giant method" or "giant class," resulting in trading one code smell for another. Managers usually exist as spillover from a class that is growing too large, where the manager is given various aggregation and collection responsibilities of the original class.

**Helper**: Classes with "Helper" in the name indicate that the original object was not up to its task and needs 'help,' and the existence of any Helper is considered a name smell.

## Related Problematic Names

Tools can set up policies warning for certain words like base, abstract, helper, manager, object.

Naming classes like CustomerService, CustomerMapper, CustomerBuilder, CustomerManager, or CustomerParser is considered an anti-pattern and a design smell.

While your search query also included "final," "real," and "new," the results focused primarily on "Manager" and "Helper" as the primary problematic naming patterns in code.

Based on the search results, here are the key findings about naming conventions and avoiding version suffixes in module names:

## Go Module Versioning Best Practice

For modules with major version ≥ 2, append /vN to the path (e.g., github.com/example/mymodule/v2). However, omit the version suffix for v0 or v1.

This convention aligns with semantic import versioning, ensuring compatibility across major releases. The reason behind this approach is that the /v2 suffix signals a major version update, and dependents explicitly import this version to avoid breaking changes.

## General Module Naming Guidelines

Beyond versioning, standard best practices recommend:

- When naming a module, use lowercase letters and underscores, making it descriptive but concise
- Stick to lowercase and hyphens for cross-platform compatibility
- Ensure paths remain stable—changes break dependencies

The key principle is that version suffixes like "v2" should be integrated into the module path structure itself (for major versions ≥ 2) rather than appended to module names informally, as this ensures proper dependency management and semantic versioning.

input.prompt: List the specific naming smells and weasel words this author identifies (e.g. words like new, real, final, base, manager, helper, data, info). Quote the key rules about what makes a name a smell and what good names look like.
Quote the naming section: what does it say about descriptive names, avoiding abbreviations, and any guidance relevant to naming by what something is/does rather than qualifiers or version markers.

Quote the naming conventions section, especially any guidance on descriptive names, the "one honking great idea" of readability, module/package naming (short lowercase names), and avoiding non-descriptive suffixes.

content.content:
# Naming Smells Identified by Peter Hilton

## Specific Naming Smells

1. **Meaningless names**: `foo`, `bar` – placeholders lacking any significance
2. **Abstract names**: `data`, `object`, `thing` – vague without indicating content type
3. **Numeric suffixes**: `employee2`, `data2`, `data_2` – unclear differentiation between variants
4. **Abbreviated names**: `acc`, `pos`, `char`, `mod`, `auth` – ambiguous due to multiple possible expansions
5. **Short names**: Single-letter variables like `a`, `i`, `j`, `k` – meta-ambiguous with unclear intent
6. **Symbolic names**: `>=>` (the "fish operator") – incomprehensible ASCII art
7. **Vague words**: `-Manager` suffix, `get` prefix, `is-` prefix on Booleans – unclear action or property
8. **Vestigial Hungarian notation**: `isVictory`, `dateCreated` – type information redundant in modern languages
9. **Multiple words**: `appointment_list`, `company_person`, `text_correction_by_editor` – concatenated concepts
10. **Wrong names**: `order`, `carrier` – contextually incorrect despite being real words

## Key Rules About Good Names

**On the universal refactoring solution:**
"All have the same refactoring: *Rename*."

**On what names should communicate:**
"Bad names lack precision or accuracy" and should carry "as much meaning as they might."

**On concreteness:**
"People find concrete examples easier to understand than abstract examples."

# Google Python Style Guide: Naming Section Summary

The naming section emphasizes clarity and descriptiveness:

**On Descriptive Names:**
"Names should be descriptive. This includes functions, classes, variables, attributes, files and any other type of named entities."

**On Avoiding Abbreviations:**
"Avoid abbreviation. In particular, do not use abbreviations that are ambiguous or unfamiliar to readers outside your project, and do not abbreviate by deleting letters within a word."

**On Type-Based Naming:**
The guide discourages including type information within variable names themselves, listing "names that needlessly include the type of the variable (for example: `id_to_name_dict`)" as something to avoid.

**General Principle:**
Rather than using version markers or qualifier suffixes, names should reflect what something does or represents. The guide recommends following established conventions (CapWords for classes, lower_with_under for functions/variables) so meaning is conveyed through naming patterns rather than descriptive suffixes.

# PEP 8 Naming Conventions Summary

## Descriptive Naming Styles

PEP 8 recognizes multiple naming conventions, including `lowercase`, `lower_case_with_underscores`, `CapitalizedWords` (also called CamelCase), and `UPPER_CASE_WITH_UNDERSCORES`. The guide notes: "When using acronyms in CapWords, capitalize all the letters of the acronym. Thus HTTPServerError is better than HttpServerError."

## Module and Package Naming

For modules and packages, the guidance is clear and practical: "Modules should have short, all-lowercase names. Underscores can be used in the module name if it improves readability. Python packages should also have short, all-lowercase names, although the use of underscores is discouraged."

## Readability as Core Principle

The document emphasizes that "code is read much more often than it is written," and names should reflect actual usage rather than implementation details. The overriding principle states: "Names that are visible to the user as public parts of the API should follow conventions that reflect usage rather than implementation."

## Key Guidance on Consistency

Rather than enforcing rigid rules, PEP 8 advises: "A style guide is about consistency. Consistency with this style guide is important. Consistency within a project is more important. Consistency within one module or function is the most important."

The document permits exceptions when guidelines would reduce readability or conflict with existing code patterns in a project.

input.prompt: Summarize the argument about why generic qualifier names like Manager/Processor/Handler are a smell, and quote the recommended alternative naming discipline.

content.content:
# The Problem with Generic Naming Suffixes

The article argues that names like `SomethingManager` are problematic because they're fundamentally vague. As Alan Green explains, "manager" has at least ten dictionary definitions ranging from "make compliant" to "achieve one's purpose," making it impossible to determine what a class actually does.

Take `UrlManager`—it could pool URLs, manipulate them, or audit their usage. The suffix tells you almost nothing concrete. More specific alternatives like `UrlBuilder` immediately clarify the class's purpose.

## The Recommended Discipline

Steve McConnell's guidelines emphasize precision:

**"Describe everything the routine does... If that makes the name ridiculously long, the name isn't the problem. Your routine is."**

The key principles include:
- Avoid vague verbs and choose specific action words
- Make names as long as necessary for clarity
- For functions, describe the return value
- Use precise opposites (`Open`/`Close`, `Insert`/`Delete`)

The underlying philosophy: if you can't name something succinctly and clearly, that's a signal the code itself needs refactoring.

Web search results for query: ""semantic versioning" module code version in package name not class name interface evolution best practice 2025"

Links: [{"title":"Python Package Versioning: SemVer, CalVer, and Best Practices","url":"https://inventivehq.com/blog/python-package-versioning-guide"},{"title":"Semantic Versioning 2.0.0 | Semantic Versioning","url":"https://semver.org/"},{"title":"Semantic Versioning Explained: Rules, Benefits & Best Practices","url":"https://talent500.com/blog/semantic-versioning-explained-guide/"},{"title":"About semantic versioning | npm Docs","url":"https://docs.npmjs.com/about-semantic-versioning/"},{"title":"Python Versioning Explained: Semantic Versioning & Package Management","url":"https://fonzi.ai/blog/python-versioning-semantic"},{"title":"Semantic Versioning Explained: A Practical SemVer Guide | Kenin Kujovic","url":"https://keninkujovic.com/blog/semantic-versioning"},{"title":"5 Best Practices for Versioning Python Packages","url":"https://blog.inedo.com/python/best-practices-for-versioning-python-packages-in-the-enterprise/"},{"title":"NuGet Best Practices and Versioning for .NET Developers | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@sweetondonie/nuget-best-practices-and-versioning-for-net-developers-cedc8ede5f16"},{"title":"From 1.0.0 to 2025.4: Making sense of software versioning — WorkOS","url":"https://workos.com/blog/software-versioning-guide"},{"title":"Semantic Versioning for APIs: A Complete Guide to SemVer Best Practices - Zuplo","url":"https://zuplo.com/learning-center/semantic-api-versioning"}]

Based on the search results, here's comprehensive information about semantic versioning and related best practices for 2025:

## Semantic Versioning Basics

Semantic versioning (SemVer) uses MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH format where MAJOR changes break compatibility, MINOR adds features backward-compatibly, and PATCH fixes bugs. A normal version number MUST take the form X.Y.Z where X, Y, and Z are non-negative integers. X is the major version, Y is the minor version, and Z is the patch version.

## Core Principles

Software using Semantic Versioning MUST declare a public API. This API could be declared in the code itself or exist strictly in documentation. However it is done, it SHOULD be precise and comprehensive.

Bug fixes not affecting the API increment the patch version, backward compatible API additions/changes increment the minor version, and backward incompatible API changes increment the major version.

## Best Practices for 2025

**Version Numbering in Packages:** Every time you make significant updates to an npm package you own, you should publish a new version of the package with an updated version number in the package.json file that follows the semantic versioning spec. Following the semantic versioning spec helps other developers who depend on your code understand the extent of changes in a given version, and adjust their own code if necessary.

**Pre-release Handling:** In practice, treat 0.y.z like 1.y.z: bump y for breaking changes, z for compatible ones. Hitting 1.0.0 should mean you are willing to be held to MAJOR/MINOR/PATCH semantics.

**Documentation & Automation:** Pair every version bump with a changelog entry. SemVer tells consumers that something changed, not what changed. A v2.0.0 jump is meaningless without a changelog explaining which APIs broke.

Use tools like semantic-release, standard-version, or release-it to automatically manage version numbers based on commit messages and changelog entries. Automation reduces human error and ensures consistency, especially when working in teams.

**Deprecation Strategy:** If you're deprecating features, always signal it clearly in the changelog and with your version number. Clear communication about deprecated features and their removal helps users transition without breaking their setups unexpectedly.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text:
I have everything I need.

## Best practice (2026): Naming discipline (no marketing adjectives)

**Core rule.** Name a thing by *what it IS or DOES*, never by a quality claim or a version marker. A name is a promise about behavior; adjectives like `honest`, `final`, `real`, `true`, `smart`, `magic`, `ultimate` are promises the code can't keep — they describe the author's *intent or mood*, not the interface. If `real_parser` exists, what was the other one? The adjective is a scar from a migration that was never finished.

### Why these words are a code smell

- **Weasel words carry no information.** Peter Hilton's *Naming Smells* classes `data`, `object`, `thing`, and `-Manager`-style suffixes as smells precisely because they don't tell the reader what the entity does; every one has the "same refactoring: *Rename*." `honest_config` and `config` denote the same type — the adjective is pure noise. ([hilton.org.uk](https://hilton.org.uk/blog/naming-smells))
- **Temporal/quality adjectives rot.** `new` becomes old, `final` gets a sequel (`final_final`), `smart` invites a `dumb` counterpart that never appears. The word encodes a moment in the repo's history, not a property of the code — so it lies the day after it's written.
- **Numeric/version suffixes are explicitly listed smells.** Hilton names `employee2`, `data2`, `data_2` as smells: the reader cannot tell what distinguishes the variants. `v2`/`r3`/`sweep` in a *symbol* name is the same defect — the differentiator lives in tribal memory, not the name.
- **Vague qualifier suffixes signal a design problem.** The `SomethingManager` critique (Atwood/McConnell): if you can't name it concretely, "the name isn't the problem, your routine is." `magic_handler` is an admission that you don't know what the code's single responsibility is. ([codinghorror.com](https://blog.codinghorror.com/i-shall-call-it-somethingmanager/))

### What the accepted conventions actually say

- **PEP 8** — modules/packages get "short, all-lowercase names"; names visible in the public API "should follow conventions that reflect *usage* rather than implementation." An adjective like `real`/`final` reflects implementation history, not usage. ([peps.python.org/pep-0008](https://peps.python.org/pep-0008/))
- **Google Python Style Guide** — "Names should be descriptive… for functions, classes, variables, attributes, files." It explicitly rejects names that encode metadata rather than meaning (e.g. `id_to_name_dict` — the `_dict` is the type, not the role), and forbids abbreviation-by-deleting-letters. The same logic bans quality tags: they encode the author's claim, not the entity's job. ([google.github.io/styleguide/pyguide](https://google.github.io/styleguide/pyguide.html))
- **Consistency hierarchy (PEP 8):** consistency within a module > within a project > with the global guide. A lone `_v2` breaks module-level consistency by definition.

### How mature teams handle versioned modules

The discipline is: **version the distribution, not the identifiers.** SemVer (`MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH`) is metadata on the *package*, declared in `pyproject.toml` / `package.json` / a git tag — never baked into a symbol name. ([semver.org](https://semver.org/), [npm docs](https://docs.npmjs.com/about-semantic-versioning/))

- **Don't** create `parser_v2.py` alongside `parser.py`. **Do** evolve `parser.py` under version control; the old behavior lives in git history and the changelog, where it belongs. "SemVer tells consumers *that* something changed" — the changelog tells them *what*.
- **When two implementations must genuinely coexist,** name them by their *distinguishing property*, not a sequence number: `StreamingParser` vs `BufferedParser`, not `Parser`/`ParserV2`. The name should answer "which one do I want?" on its own.
- **For a hard, breaking API generation** (the one case a version marker is legitimate), scope it in a **package/namespace boundary**, not a symbol suffix: `api/v1/…` and `api/v2/…` as directories (mirroring Go's `module/v2` semantic-import-versioning convention), so the old surface stays importable during migration and the version is a namespace, not an adjective glued to a class.
- **Interfaces over forks:** stabilize a protocol/ABC (`Storage`) and let implementations vary behind it (`S3Storage`, `LocalStorage`) rather than shipping `Storage` + `StorageNew`.

### A concrete renaming discipline

1. **Grep the ban list** as a CI gate. Fail the build on any file/symbol matching `honest|final|real|true|smart|magic|ultimate|\bnew\b|\bold\b|v\d+|r\d+|sweep` in identifier position.
2. **For each hit, ask the two-question test:** (a) *What does this actually do?* → rename to that. (b) *What distinguishes it from its sibling?* → if nothing, delete the loser; if something, name it after that property.
3. **Kill the sibling.** An adjectived name almost always implies a stale twin (`config` vs `honest_config`). Finish the migration: delete the old one, drop the adjective from the survivor.
4. **One rename per commit**, mechanical and reviewable; let the tool do the edit so blame stays legible.

### Named tools

- **Ruff** / **flake8-builtins** / **pep8-naming (`pep8-naming`/`N8xx`)** — enforce PEP 8 casing and flag shadowing.
- **Ruff `flake8-tidy-imports` banned-api** and generic linters like **Semgrep** / **grep-based pre-commit hooks** — encode a project-specific banned-word list (`base`, `helper`, `manager`, `util`, `v2`, `final`, `real`) as a custom rule; this is the standard way teams hard-fail weasel words.
- **IDE "Rename Symbol"** (LSP rename) / **`git mv`** — the refactor for *every* naming smell is the same operation: Rename.
- **`pydeprecate` / `warnings.deprecated` (PEP 702)** — the sanctioned way to retire an old API without minting a `_v2` twin: keep the name, deprecate the behavior, bump MAJOR.

### What "good" looks like

`StreamingJSONParser`, `retry_with_backoff`, `S3ArtifactStore`, `api/v2/routes.py` — every name states a role or a distinguishing property and would still read correctly to a stranger with zero repo history. Nothing in the name is a claim about quality (`smart`, `honest`), a boast (`ultimate`), or a timestamp (`new`, `v3`, `sweep`). If removing a word from a name loses no meaning, the word was a smell.

---
**Sources:** [Naming smells — Peter Hilton](https://hilton.org.uk/blog/naming-smells) · [PEP 8](https://peps.python.org/pep-0008/) · [Google Python Style Guide](https://google.github.io/styleguide/pyguide.html) · [I Shall Call It… SomethingManager — Coding Horror](https://blog.codinghorror.com/i-shall-call-it-somethingmanager/) · [Semantic Versioning 2.0.0](https://semver.org/) · [About semantic versioning — npm](https://docs.npmjs.com/about-semantic-versioning/)
